data.title: Ryanair: Elon Musk Threatens To Buy In Starlink Spat
data.description:
Three weeks into the year, 2026 is already shaping up to be an eventful year. One item that I did not have on my checklist for 2026 was a feud between Ryanair (RYAAY) and Elon Musk, the world’s richest man. Yet, that is precisely what is happening. Musk and Ryanair are entangled in a public feud over the airline’s decision not to install Starlink on its fleet of 643 airplanes. Ryanair believes that installation and drag penalties would be too high to make it a commercial success. Musk disagrees and polled his followers on X to acquire Ryanair. In this report, I discuss the economics of installation and comment on any takeover of Ryanair by Elon Musk. I have a "Buy" rating on Ryanair, and we see that the stock has gained 14.2% since my last report while upside from FY26 earnings has not yet been priced in.

Ryanair Starlink Economics

I have modeled the Ryanair Starlink economics with fees ranging from $2 to $20. The revenue potential ranges between $400 million and $4 billion. Typically, around 6%-8% of the passengers would convert, which would yield around $24-$320 million in revenues. However, we note that Ryanair’s passengers are flying with Ryanair for its low cost. Ryanair boss O’Leary expects less than 5% to actually convert. This limits the revenue potential to $20-$200 million.

Recurring costs would be $140.5 million when we apply the numbers to Ryanair’s latest full-year results, including a $122.1 million penalty, which is less severe than the $200 million penalty that the company expects. In our assessment, I am assuming a discounted rate of $2,500 in recurring subscription fees and maintenance fees per aircraft per month. In that assessment, we note that at a price point of $10 and 8% conversion, there is a profit before installation costs. From $14 million onward, we see a more solid path for consistent profitability. If we include non-recurring costs, we get to break-even ranging between eight and 69 months, depending on the pricing and conversion rate. I believe that for investments like these, Ryanair would typically look for a return on investment larger than 100%, and that only happens from a price point higher than $16 and at an 8% conversion rate. So, for Ryanair, there does not seem to be an attractive business case, as 8% is the upper limit for conversion and unlikely to be reflective of conversion on low-cost, short-haul flights. Starlink is capable, but for Ryanair’s business, they would need high prices, and in some cases, the internet access fee would exceed Ryanair’s base fares.

Starlink believes the actual fuel burn impact would be closer to 0.3%. If we implement that, we see Starlink is already profitable when priced at $4 and a conversion rate of 5%. However, we do note that return on investment will only exceed 100% starting at $14 at a conversion rate of 6%. For a 5% conversion rate, a $16 fee would be required at least to satisfy the ROI>100% criterion. I still believe that it is unlikely that Starlink is the product that could be rolled out within Ryanair with a good return on investment. It would take a discount on the hardware and installation for Ryanair to be interesting, and perhaps that is also what Ryanair is after. They are not after increasing their brand value or reputation on a service level but are interested in keeping costs low, and that also includes costs for acquiring Starlink terminals.

Can Musk Really Buy Ryanair?

The feud between Ryanair is happening in the open and might actually be entertaining, with Musk polling his followers on whether he should buy Ryanair and replace the CEO and O’Leary dedicating an entire press conference to the economics of Starlink. While my assessment somewhat differs from the estimates of O’Leary, they do support O’Leary’s conclusion. At the same time, there are two items to note. The first one is that O'Leary tends to play things via the media to generate leverage in negotiations with suppliers. He is known for that on Boeing airplanes, as are many other airline executives who want to order Boeing or Airbus jets, and he also frequently threatens to halt routes when airport fees are not low enough. So, somewhere I do believe that Ryanair would like to have Starlink to drive ancillary revenues, but they want it at a substantially lower price point.

The second item to note is that acquiring Twitter, now X, also seemed to have started out as a joke and eventually became a reality in a $44 billion deal. Ryanair currently has a market cap of $35 billion. If you add a 30% premium, you would end up north of $45.5 billion. So, Musk could seemingly buy Ryanair for a price comparable to Twitter.

However, from a regulatory point of view, it is unlikely that this could really happen. EU ownership rules dictate that airlines must be majority owned by EU or EFTA nationals or corporations with effective EU control. So, that limits Musk to a minority stake.

In December 2020, Ryanair announced that voting restrictions pursuant to EU Regulation 1008/2008 continue to hold, meaning that non-EU owners of Ryanair shares cannot attend, speak, or vote at general meetings. That effectively means that even if Musk really wanted to buy shares, he could, but he could not vote for changes, including a change of leadership.

Conclusion: Ryanair Is Doing The Usual Ryanair Things

In some way, Ryanair is doing the usual thing. The company evaluates the appeal of an investment decision from an ROI perspective. If that is not to the company’s liking, they simply do not carry on with negotiations and instead take things to the media if necessary to generate leverage. The Starlink feud seems to be no different. It may result in Ryanair eventually getting Starlink at the price point it wants. If not, only a competitor moving in the direction of Starlink on low-cost carriers could force Ryanair’s hand to do the same thing. So, only if Ryanair is set to lose an edge to draw in cost-conscious customers or would lose a cost advantage against competitors, as was the case with Wizz Air’s upgauging move to the A321neo forcing Ryanair to eventually buy the MAX 10, would we expect Ryanair to get Starlink even at the undesired price, but not before that. I currently see no reason to change my "Buy" rating on the name.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Trinity Capital: 6 Factors Positioning This 13% Yielding BDC As A Prime Buy For 2026
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Despite the additional pressure on the Business Development Companies (BDC) sector due to the Fed's rate cut cycle, one of the few assets in this sector that I recommend "Buy" is Trinity Capital (TRIN). I will discuss 6 key factors that will allow the company to maintain its high dividend yield while demonstrating share price growth. Even though this is an asset that's good for income-focused investors, lower stock prices reduce the total return. This is why I always try to add BDCs that aren't losing value, because sooner or later, you might need the capital invested in them to rebalance your portfolio or use it for other investment goals.

Factor #1: Adjusting the Business Model to the Cycle of Falling Interest Rates

The TRIN business model is based on venture lending to innovative small and medium-sized businesses. Although this form of financing is non-bank in nature, the creditworthiness of potential borrowers is assessed more strictly. As a result, careful selection is made between applications for specific projects, with a smaller share of capital being allocated first, then the remainder. It's a really reliable way to build a strong loan portfolio, though it does limit how much you can scale up. Lowering the Fed's rates is making it easier for the venture capital sector to get investment, and more projects are becoming eligible for non-bank financing. It means a potential increase in the number of new customers who could become potential borrowers for TRIN. The company's one-time expansion of its loan portfolio through an increase in the flow of applications from borrowers that have a better credit rating is the main factor ensuring the strategic development of the business. In this way, TRIN's adaptation of its business model to the current cycle of falling interest rates is an undoubted advantage for the company in 2026.

Factor #2: Additional Income From Warrants

Apart from focusing on venture lending, the TRIN business model includes the potential to earn not only interest income but also income from warrants. The financial instrument means that the company can make a profit if the borrower goes public. The main reason for this is the growth in the borrower's market value. Right now, the TRIN portfolio has 210 warrant positions in 133 companies. According to GAAP, total potential profit from warrants for Q3 2025 amounted to approximately $70.1 million. Assume that only 50% of the company's warrants will be monetized; their value upon exercise will be $68.5 million.

Factor #3: Scale Effect From Internal Management

TRIN is a BDC company that adheres to an internal management model, with the advantage of economies of scale. With an increase in the loan portfolio, the company does not need to increase its expenses on external advisors. According to the median for the BDC sector, external management results in a loss of approximately 2% of asset value and 20% of profits to pay for such external advisors. Because TRIN does not have these additional expenses, they can use the funds saved for additional bonuses and premiums for their employees, thus increasing their motivation. As the AUM of the portfolio grows, operational expenses grow more slowly. This means that the bigger the TRIN portfolio and the more money it makes, that's how much more cash shareholders get. It's also important to have good internal management because you need to make quick decisions, as venture lending needs a lot of flexibility. To give you an example, at big companies like Ares Capital (ARCC) and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), there can be internal conflicts between managers when outside advisors don't like the decisions made by BDC managers. This can cause delays in funding potential borrowers, who are usually innovative companies, leading to a loss of customers.

Factor #4: Equipment Financing Model Through Leasing

The thing that makes TRIN more unique is its financing model, with a separate part going to equipment. As of Q3 2025, 14.5% of the company's loan portfolio is made up of equipment leasing. At the beginning of 2025, the share was 18.8%, which is still a significant portion of funds secured by collateral rather than just intellectual property rights. To provide this type of financing, TRIN purchases servers, equipment, and technology, then transfers them to borrowers on a leasing basis. Besides providing real collateral for venture loans for the company, there's another perk to this financing model: it helps diversify its sources of income. In addition to interest, at the end of the contract, it can make extra money from the residual value of the equipment. Leasing equipment also gives it extra tax benefits through depreciation.

Factor #5: Preservation of Alpha Dividend Yield

TRIN is an asset with a high dividend yield. Currently, its forward yield is 12.67%. Per-share dividends are $0.51, although there has been no increase since Q1 2024. The payout ratio is 102%, and this figure has not changed since Q1 2025. We should also add that in December 2025, TRIN switched from quarterly to monthly payments, and this will definitely make this asset more attractive to investors looking not just to get a steady passive income but also to live off it. However, this is not an advantage, taking into account the dynamics of changes in dividend coverage and the fact that dividends have not increased since 2024. It would be incorrect to say that TRIN's dividend yield is insufficient. A yield of 12.67% is still very high, given that the market is experiencing a systemic decline in the yields of various payments, including ETFs, CEFs, and other BDCs, as a result of the rate-cutting cycle. As an example, the dividend yield of Hercules Capital (HTGC), a major competitor, was 10.09%.

Factor #6: Technical Analysis Signals Continued Stock Growth

Even though TRIN is primarily an asset that's good for income-focused investors, the stock is still pretty volatile. Thanks to the company's effective continued development and scaling of its business, market value will keep going up. This is why I want to highlight TRIN's prospects through technical analysis of higher time frames. In the 1D chart, for example, you can see the price moving within an upward trend channel, whose lower boundary was tested for strength at the end of 2025. The channel kept the price within its boundaries, giving TRIN the potential to continue growing to the upper boundary ($17.50).

Looking at the 1W chart, there is a similar situation, with TRIN shares in an upward trend channel. This upper limit is around $17.70. As such, from the current level, it is possible for TRIN to increase in value by 8-10%. This potential share price growth could be realized by the end of 2026. Given the dividend yield, estimated at 11%–13% throughout the year, Total Return on investments in TRIN will reach 19%–23%.

TRIN Risks

Analyzing the risks of investing in TRIN, first and foremost, the continued negative impact of the Fed's rate-cutting cycle on the economic conditions of the BDC sector should be taken into account. Between Q3 2024 and Q3 2025, variable-rate venture loans increased from 76.6% to 82.4%, resulting in a possible decline in net investment income. This impact should be limited, however, since the venture market has historically performed better when interest rates are falling. Therefore, over the long term, venture lending activity is likely to pick up again. Due to this, loan risks will remain at the same minimal level, though the TRIN portfolio has never been characterized by a high proportion of non-performing loans. The level of defaulted loans in the portfolio for Q3 2025 is 1.0%, whereas in Q3 2024 the figure was 1.4%.

In addition, I would pay special attention to the risk associated with switching to monthly dividend payments. In one sense, the decision is positive, which increases TRIN's investment appeal for investors focused on receiving regular income. However, this will make it more difficult for the company to maneuver its capital during periods of increased market volatility. This could result in adjustments to the size of dividends, a move that investors are likely to react to negatively. Considering TRIN's leverage ratio of 1.19x, the situation could become more serious.

Conclusion

In summary, the 6 factors identified above determine TRIN's high investment attractiveness despite the fact that the BDC sector suffers from the negative impact of the rate-cutting cycle. I recommend «Buy» for this company's shares. My expectation is that over the course of 2026, TRIN's Total Return could be 19%–23%, of which half will be mandatory payments and the other half will be growth potential to the upper limit of the bullish trend channel.

data.title: Riot Platforms: AMD Today, Many AI Players Tomorrow
data.description:
Riot Platforms, Inc. (RIOT) is a bitcoin (BTC-USD) mining company that chose to ride the AI ​​wave. I don't see the same focus that Bitfarms Ltd. (BITF) has been using, a company I analyzed in a recent article. BITF is choosing a total transition from cryptocurrency mining to infrastructure for AI and HPC infrastructure. RIOT, on the other hand, is reconverting its three large mining farms into facilities to host AI services, but for the time being, it will not abandon Bitcoin mining.

Even more, what I like the most about RIOT is that it's been using its bitcoin for that conversion. On January16th it announced the purchase of 200 acres at the Rockdale facilities, Texas, for almost $96 million derived from the sale of 1,080 bitcoin.

The announcement meant not only an extension of its operational capabilities, but also an agreement with Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) to supply 25 MW during the first quarter 2026 with the possibility to expand the buying to 200 MW. The contract extends for 10 years and the company expects long-term revenues of $311 million, with the possibility of expanding revenues to $1 billion if all extensions are exercised.

I think this was a great announcement for the company, and I hope it will be the first of many semiconductor companies or companies linked to AI development that will require RIOT's services. I hope that because I am rating the company as a Buy due to its future potential thanks to its electrical load management (which differentiates it from data center REITs) and because its installed capacity is not so far from reaching 1.7 or 1.8 GW based on its projections.

The Advantage Of Buying Electricity

Flexible management of electricity purchases for bitcoin miners is a key point in my investment thesis. In my previous articles I talked about how the possible increase in energy prices led me to buy companies with the adequate infrastructure to take advantage of that future potential, as could be the case of TransAlta Corporation (TAC). In fact, part of my thesis in that article was based on the deregulation of the electric market in Alberta.

In the case of RIOT, this deregulation scheme is already working because bitcoin miners are using cheap energy in moments of lower demand, and to re-sell the energy they have bought in moments of higher demand. This is explained by the company on its own website, in the window about network support.

Bitcoin miners, due to the network’s decentralized nature, can easily adjust power usage, unlike traditional data centers and manufacturers. Miners are the ideal consumer of new power generation because they provide a consistent, baseload customer during periods of low demand for power and also provide immediate, low-cost, demand-side flexibility during peak hours.

For many experts on this subject this could sound repetitive. But for an investor like myself, who is not an energy engineer, for example, it is very important to me. This is one of the advantages to keep the bitcoin mining business, which of course will have the inherent volatility risks associated with a digital currency, but I believe the benefits will outweigh the risks.

The Company Will Remain A Leading Producer Of Bitcoin

On the one hand, I believe that not abandoning bitcoin mining will continue to be a fantastic source of liquidity. I reached that conclusion after the effective use of bitcoin for the extension of Rockdale facilities. Later, I'll talk about the company's financing for its capital investments, and I'll explain why I believe it is a risk that could postpone the upside of the stock.

On the other hand, the advantage of remaining operating in the cryptocurrency market for RIOT is that it boasts a large installed mining capacity of 36.5 EH/s, as reported in Q3 2025, specifically on slide 44 of the 10-Q report. In Q3 2024 the company had 17.9 EH/s, an increase of 103%.

For those who are not familiar with this terminology, the higher the EH/s, the more efficient the company will be at mining bitcoin while using less electricity. The expectation is to reach 38.5 EH/s in Q4 2024 and 43.5 EH/s in Q1 2026. Therefore, I feel that RIOT can maintain attractive margins in cryptocurrency while transforming into a premium AI hosting provider.

In the EH/s analysis we must consider another key element: the global metric because if it goes up a lot, RIOT will be left with less bitcoin. It's an element of constant volatility, but I think the company knows how it works. I'll rephrase it: I am a bitcoin investor, ergo I also like mining. Then, if your savings are in gold or silver, I believe you'll be interested too in the mining companies of each metal.

AMD Is Just The First Tenant: RIOT's Potential To Become A Premium AI Hosting Provider

I believe RIOT will position as a great player in AI data centers because it already has the infrastructure needed to do it, and the connection with AMD is an example of that. If AMD was quick to sign a contract with the company, why wouldn't more similar companies follow suit and hire its services?

The installed capacity of RIOT could reach 1.8 GW, and that's why it is a very attractive option for semiconductors, which need a powerful digital infrastructure in data centers to supply the demand of hyperscalers. The company's portfolio is based on three big facilities: Kentucky (162 MW), Rockdale (700 MW) and Corsicana (1000 MW).

The largest power generation facilities are in Corsicana and Rockdale, although the three facilities need a strong capital investment to extend its capabilities. In fact, Kentucky, due to its operational capacity of 162 MW, actually has a potential capacity of 300 MW. Capital investment for 2025 was estimated at approximately $450 million across all improvements. Corsicana's facilities accounted for the majority of investment spending in 2025, excluding cryptocurrency mining costs.

Corsicana represents 925 acres in Texas and, with the building development, Core & Shell, the company expects to accelerate the speed of adaptation and complete a custom-built data center by 2027 (slide 12 of the Q3 Presentation). The other key establishment is Rockdale, the one that will host AMD and where the company just bought an additional 200 acres. The capacity to supply 25 MW for the first quarter of 2026 (and maybe 75 MW based on the agreement), convinced me that RIOT has the potential for rapid, phased deliveries. As I said at the beginning, there's no need to invest from scratch in a new data center, and the bulk of the investment, as you can see in the image above, it's still crypto mining.

The Strategic Location Of Texas

RIOT is the owner of almost 1,100 acres and most of it located in Texas (Corsicana and Rockdale), according to the statement of the agreement with AMD. Besides, it has a direct interconnection to an electrical network (which also belongs to RIOT, an operational 700 MW substation). This decreases the dependence on local energy.

The locations of both facilities are also strategic because they are in the Texas Triangle, a very close position to technology hubs such as Austin and Dallas. Besides, Texas is the state with the most data centers of the country, only behind Virginia. Texas is home to several large data center projects, including the establishment of OpenAI and Oracle's Project Stargate in the city of Abilene, 180 miles from Dallas.

For its part, Texas also has some cost challenges for its warm weather, but liquid cooling is playing a key role to avoid extreme heat generated by high-intensity AI servers, combined with high ambient temperatures. I believe RIOT has resolved this challenge thanks to its solid infrastructure and because it has a specialized cooling operator like Kelvion, which manages the Rockdale and Corsicana facilities.

What I Took Away From The Q3 Results And What I Expect From BTC In 2026

The analysis of Q3 2025 results was a little late for me, but it helped me understand the company's revenue model so far. Bitcoin mining remains a strong total source of income. In Q3 it was $160 million out of a total of $180 million, while in the first 9 months of 2025 it was $444 million out of a total of $494 million.

The improvement in EBIT and net income is an important factor in convincing me of my investment, but not necessarily the most crucial. Both metrics, along with revenue, you can see marked in yellow in the image of the 10-Q.

Why did I say that profitability is important, but not fundamental? Because I'm thinking about RIOT as a long-term investment. Historically, RIOT suffers from the volatility of the bitcoin price, and profitability is relatively new to its balance sheets. In fact, just in 2024 its earnings were $109 million. In the first 9 months of 2025, net income was $27 million. I'm not expecting this metric to grow too much in 2026, and it could even fall due to investments destined to facilities improvements, and even due to the volatility of the price of bitcoin.

For 2026, BTC projections vary widely between lows and highs, with an approximate floor of $75,000 and a ceiling of $225,000. Costs per bitcoin in Q3 were $89,074 (see slide 45 of the 10-Q), representing an approximate gross margin of 21-22%. BTC experienced some sharp declines in the last quarter of 2025 from its highs of $121,000 and for now, seems to be far from that figure. If BTC trends downwards in 2026, to a minimum of $75,000, margins will shrink sharply if the company does not lower its mining costs.

Anyway, I took into account the cost of depreciation of machines, which represents almost 50% of the cost per bitcoin. If RIOT extends the useful life of its machines, the margins could remain strong. Energy efficiency could also be improved through new investments, so even at the $75,000 minimum, I don't think it's a serious problem for my investment thesis.

Long-Term Upside Potential In The Stock Price

The company operates in a volatile sector that relies heavily on the price of Bitcoin to determine its margins. By comparing its ratios with those of the sector, the company might seem to be a little bit expensive, especially considering share dilution and the volatility of its returns. But compared to its ratios over the last few years, I think RIOT is cheap.

The stock is trading at 10 times its sales, compared to the 5-year average of 18 times. The EV/EBITDA ratio is 21.89% below the industry median and extremely cheap compared to its historical average. The Price-to-Book of the last 12 months is also 47.32% below the sector average.

Meanwhile, the stock is trading at 47 times its earnings compared to 37 times for the sector. I consider this could be due to the recent increase from the rise in BTC and even the recent agreement with AMD. Anyway, I believe its Price to Earnings ratio is not the ideal metric for a company that is betting for huge future income.

As I explained throughout this article, I believe the stock is also cheap regarding its potential: trading in a range of $19-$22. Below that range, I consider it a good long-term buying opportunity. Furthermore, revenue projections, based solely on the AMD announcement, already estimate constant revenues of $1 billion. Other analyses also project total revenues of $1.6 billion by 2028.

These projections fit very well with my bullish perspective due to the additional revenue from AI. Even more: in the following quarters the company will probably incorporate a revenue line in its earnings presentations related to AI infrastructure. Although AMD revenue already has a very good projection, I'm betting on seeing new contracts in the future.

Based on the agreement with AMD, the supply capacity could be expanded up to 200 MW at Rockdale. But even in that case, there's still a remaining capacity just in Rockdale of 500 MW, and another 1 GW in Corsicana. If a contract similar to AMD's is signed in 2026 alone, it could reach the $1.6 billion estimate before 2028.

I took into account that the increased use of installed capacity for AI data centers will reduce a little bit the energy required to min Bitcoin, but I believe that's precisely what the market will reward. In fact, that's something that has me very excited. I think the news about a new contract could have a very positive impact on the stock price and could lead to an upside of at least $24 or $25. My estimate is even below the analysts' consensus.

The Risks Of Financing Through Share Issuance

Issuing shares is a common practice for the company to finance its operations without resorting to excessive debt or the volatility of bitcoin. Given the maintenance of mining machines is expensive, the company needs a lot of cash flow. Now, with the adaptation to a first-rate energy infrastructure for AI data centers, the need for financing will continue to exist. That's why the past shared issuance rate caught my attention, and the trend I saw was deceleration. The year-on-year growth in issuance over the last 3 years is lower and more stable than the 2019-2021 period.

I think this is a risk because the future perspective points out an issuance of more than $500 million, so 2026 could be another year of stock dilution if mining margins do not improve.

Anyway, I don't think this is an issuance to cover operating losses or risks of failure, ergo I don't think it would bring problems to the business. The dilution is solely to create more value in the future. And I believe that the future value from AI data centers is worthwhile.

Conclusions

I am a fan of crypto, and I'm very passionate about the companies behind the phenomenon. RIOT Platforms is one of them, and I believe is one of the more efficient miners of the market. However, my article wasn't specifically about bitcoin, but about the step the company is taking to become a key player in the AI ​​data center market.

What caught my attention, then, was the idea of investing in the factories, not only in the final product. I think RIOT has the capacity to provide key services to AI players, and the agreement with AMD is a demonstration of my thesis. I hope that, over time, there will be more news about big technological companies requiring RIOT's services, especially because its infrastructure is already installed, it has a robust electrical supply network and intelligent energy procurement management, and liquid cooling to prevent high-temperature stress.

I believe RIOT could be a risky investment both in the short and medium term, but since I'm investing for the long term, I consider the risks to be lower. AI is a reality, and the infrastructure that will keep it alive will come, to a large extent, from companies like RIOT, which will complement their volatile revenues with stable income thanks to their high-intensity data centers. I think a big part of that strategy is already underway, and that's why I'm investing in its potential.

data.title: Commerce Bancshares: Excess Capital Makes Shares Attractive
data.description:
Shares of Commerce Bancshares (CBSH) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 12% of their value. Investors have been particularly concerned about its credit quality, as it has built reserves and other banks have faced large loss events in private credit portfolios. Solid Q4 results should help to allay these fears as CBSH now turns to integrating strategic M&A. Believing these risks overdone, I upgraded shares to a “Buy” in November, and that call has played out so far with the stock returning 10%. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit CBSH.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Commerce Bancshares earned $1.01, which beat estimates by $0.02 as revenue grew by 6.5% to $449 million. As previously communicated, Commerce completed its acquisition of FineMark on Jan. 1. This acquisition deepens CBSH’s wealth management offerings in Florida while also adding a presence in Arizona and South Carolina to expand beyond CBSH’s retail banking operation in the Midwest. This was a bolt-on deal, increasing CBSH’s size by about 10% and adding ~$8.7 billion of trust AUM. I view it as highly strategic, given CBSH’s focus on the wealth market and the added scale in the quickly growing Florida market. FineMark financials were not included in its Q4 release but will be going forward.

Balance Sheet Growth Continues

Turning first to its balance sheet, deposits grew by 3% to $25.6 billion. I was encouraged to see the $200 million increase in noninterest-bearing balances (“NIB”). This no-cost funding is highly valuable in today’s rate environment, and these balances faced structural pressure for several years. It does appear that balances have bottomed, which should help to protect margins going forward. Total costs fell by 6 bps sequentially to 1.14%; this is a comparatively slow decline given the Fed rate cuts. This is partly because its deposit rates were already below peers, and so there is likely less room to cut rates while still growing balances and maintaining competitiveness.

Loans were up 3.2% from last year to $17.7 billion. This loan growth has been entirely focused on commercial sectors, with consumer loans flat. Given Fed rate cuts, loan yields fell by 18 bps sequentially to 5.84%. Business loans were up 6% to $6.4 billion, while CBSH allowed lower-yielding residential mortgage balances to decline. We have seen fairly modest growth in its commercial real estate portfolio, which is about 20% of the portfolio.

As you can see below, a third of this portfolio is owner-occupied, which is a quasi-corporate exposure. Outside of this, its portfolio is fairly diverse, though it does have $500 million of office exposure, which is a key focus area. Importantly, CBSH has very conservative underwriting. In fact, not a single office loan is currently delinquent, and with a loan-to-value of just 64%, there is a substantial equity cushion that makes potential losses less likely. While CRE is likely to see elevated losses across the sector again in 2026, I am comfortable with CBSH’s positioning.

Fixed Reinvestment Boosts Margins

CBSH has a comparatively low 69% loan-to-deposit ratio, leaving it with a fairly large securities portfolio of $9.5 billion. This portfolio’s size has been stable over the past year as management focuses on growing loans. Its investment portfolio has a 2.96% yield, and with a 4.3-year duration, it will take time for this portfolio to roll over entirely and for its $508 million unrealized loss to work down. That said, there are $1.2 billion of fixed-rate paydowns and maturities in 2026, and CBSH should be able to redeploy those funds at higher yields, boosting net interest income by at least $12 million on a run-rate basis.

Net interest income was $283 million, which was up 6% from last year. Its net interest margin (“NIM”) compressed 4 bps from Q3 to 3.6% given the modest change in deposit rates. NIM was still up 11 bps from a year ago, thanks to lower funding costs and the benefits of fixed reinvestment. As its fixed income portfolio continues to roll over and CBSH invests at current market yields, I see scope for modest continued NIM accretion in 2206, assuming 2 Fed rate cuts.

Fee revenue grew 7% from last year to $166 million as rising markets lift AUM and advisory fees. Trust fees were up 10%, while consumer brokerage services were up 12%. I would note that CBSH generated about $180 million in card transaction fees. There is a push in Congress to cut interchange fees, though passage of legislation is highly uncertain. This could be a ~$20 million risk of CBSH’s business, or about $0.11 per share. While smaller than credit card operators, this exposure is a bit higher than other regional banks and could risk 2%-3% of earnings.

Credit Quality and Capital Are Strong

Credit quality was encouraging. Net charge-offs remain muted at 0.22%, down 1 bp from Q3. The bank has just $16 million of non-accrual loans, flat sequentially. Against this, it has $180 million of allowances for losses, or ~11x nonaccruals, which is an extremely healthy coverage ratio that protects it against a potential rise in defaults. Even as credit costs have stayed muted, CBSH has steadily added reserves, which now cover 1.01% of loans. I am comfortable with CBSH’s reserve positioning and view it as relatively conservative.

Beyond solid reserves, Commerce maintains an industry-leading CET1 capital ratio of 17.5%, providing ample capacity for buybacks, growth, and strategic M&A like the FineMark purchase. Even factoring in its unrealized loss on investment securities, its capital ratio would be over 15%, which is an extremely healthy level as CBSH does not really need much more than 10% capital to manage its business.

Indeed, given its capital positioning, CBSH took advantage of the decline in shares to buy back 2.2 million shares during Q4. Thanks to repurchases, its share count was down nearly 2% from last year, and I see scope for an increased pace of buybacks over 2026. Additionally, shares offer a 2% dividend yield. Commerce has grown its dividend 8 straight years, and I expect ongoing dividend growth in 2026 and beyond.

Shares Are Attractive; Reiterate "Buy"

Back in November, I was targeting $4.65 in 2026 EPS and estimated Commerce’s excess capital at about $5/share. While there can be some purchase accounting noise in Q1 related to its FineMark purchase, I still see CBSH tracking towards this, +/- $0.20, assuming 2 rate cuts and a stable equity market. I also expect its share count to decline by up to 5% over the course of the year, so long as shares remain near current levels. Even after its run, shares are trading at less than 12x earnings, which I view as attractive given its leading capital position, under-levered balance sheet, and ongoing fixed reinvestment tailwinds. I view fair value as being about $60 (12x earnings plus $5 of excess capital), for a ~12% return potential including the dividend. As such, I remain CBSH as a “Buy” and view it as one of the highest quality opportunities in the regional banking sector.

data.title: SunPower: Upside, But Not A Buy
data.description:
Thesis

SunPower Inc. (SPWR) was historically a U.S.-based company that sold, serviced, and installed residential solar energy systems. After years of financial trouble capped by a Chapter 11 filing and a Nasdaq delisting, SunPower’s remaining assets were sold off, and the buyer kept the name, looking to reboot the brand. After digging through the latest earnings, I’m staying neutral. Gains still lean on deal-making and lucky timing.

Scale, Financing, and Cyclical Risk

  • Revenue set a record of $88.5 million, up from $70 million last quarter.
  • Q1 '26 revenue is expected to be $84 million, and I say it's highly uncertain.
  • We’ve gone from 1,100 to 2,000 rounded out, almost double in our sales rep headcount.
  • Another company in Salt Lake went bankrupt. It's called Purolite. We brought in about 350 Purolite guys.

SunPower’s fourth quarter looks better than you’d expect, given how chaotic solar has been. The company's started this year on a relatively strong note. A lot of that seems tied to acquisitions. In particular, bringing in Sunder and Ambia expanded its footprint and simply put more boots on the ground. More reps usually means more deals, and the expansion into additional states (the obvious “sunbelt” markets, but also places where summer bills are brutal) likely helped keep demand from cooling off too much.

What’s interesting is why the demand held up. Customers are starting to drift toward TPO (third-party) ownership and leases. The main reason is they’re easier to finance. California is a clear example of this trend. When a PG&E bill’s already ulcer-inducing, a lease that knocks it down a tad can seem safer than coughing up a large cash payment upfront. That shift also creates an opening for a bigger player like SunPower to pick up share from smaller, local installers who don’t have as much financing reach or marketing scale.

Still, the sector may be heading into a slowdown, especially now that the One Big Beautiful Bill has accelerated the end of the 30% residential investment tax credit after last year. That shift probably pulled demand forward. In other words, customers rushed to buy in the short term, almost like a panic, because they wanted systems installed before the incentive stepped down. As a result, this year is likely dealing with the hangover from that rush. In that setup, a few experts think a 20% to 30% pullback isn’t crazy. That’s especially true for cash buyers, since the payback math suddenly looks a lot worse. Financing can fill part of the gap, but it doesn’t erase the sticker shock. Even so, prepaid leases and other TPO options could help cushion the blow.

Competition also gets uglier in a TPO-heavy market. Sunrun’s (RUN) move to lean hard into leases is the obvious pressure point, and deals like its NRG Energy (NRG) partnership, bundling solar with broader utility-style services, could chip away at SunPower’s direct-sales advantage. To be fair, bundling isn’t an automatic win; customers, me included, don’t always want one more thing showing up on the bill.

  • On the gross margin, we had exceptional gross margin because we cleaned up some old backlog that we bought from SunPower.
  • We took $1.1 million out of this number and stuffed it into reserves.

Meanwhile, working through inherited backlog appears to have helped gross margin, converting older inventory into revenue without having to load up on new product at today’s prices. That’s a real plus. But it can also be the kind of tailwind that makes you look healthier than you actually are once it fades. Meanwhile, supply chains are normalizing in this tariff-era of Trump distortions, and that doesn’t necessarily mean prices stay firm. If anything, commoditization keeps marching on. With more U.S. manufacturing capacity online, some of it tied to Asian firms using local assembly to work around duties, panel pricing could soften again.

Operational Leverage and Financial Position

  • Our operating income is a new SunPower record of $3.5 million.
  • That's only 4% of revenue.
  • This quarter, we're setting a record first time we've been over $400,000 per employee per year.
  • That number right here, $285,000 per employee per year is our breakeven level.

Profitability just hit a high-water mark, mostly because the company ran a pretty tight operation. The acquisitions helped too. When you combine teams, you can eliminate duplicate job roles and share fixed costs such as finance, human resources, and warehousing across a larger sales operation. And it shows up in the revenue-per-employee number, which jumped, helped by a bigger sales push without a corresponding surge in W-2 hires.

On the competitive side, Enphase (ENPH), for example, appears to be pushing hard on both the hardware and the surrounding stack, things like newer inverter designs (including gallium nitride work) plus software that ties together home batteries and EV charging. If that ecosystem becomes harder to leave, SunPower’s combined hardware-and-software offering could start to look less compelling, especially if important partnerships either don’t happen or stop being exclusive. To be fair, I don't think that every customer wants a fully integrated “everything app” for their home energy. I don't. The simpler, the better, I say.

  • Our ending cash balance was $9.3 million, up from $5.1 million in the prior quarter.
  • We increased our equity line of credit with White Line Capital to $55 million on January 11.
  • We have made a promise to investors that we're going to have minimum cash of $10 million.

Liquidity improved in the meantime thanks to a bigger equity line of credit. That buys them some room to maneuver without having to dilute right away, which is the good part. The less-comforting read is that leaning on an equity line can be a signal management doesn’t want to (or can’t) tap cheaper capital right now.

For the longer term, SunPower is talking about reaching $1 billion in revenue by 2028. The part I’d watch closely is how they get there. They expect to get there through acquisitions and incremental product changes, like bifacial panels and more fully integrated systems, basically continuing along a path they already know well. Ambitious, not impossible.

Valuation

SPWR seems cheaper than a few other solar companies. Its stock trades at 1.27x EV/Sales, both based on past results and expected future results. By comparison, the overall solar sector typically trades around 2.4x EV/Sales.

Competitors are even higher, with ENPH trading at ~3x and Sunrun trading at ~8.0x.

The discount isn’t totally mysterious. Solar is one of those industries where a single policy change can alter the mood overnight, and SunPower’s story right now is basically to buy growth, stitch it together, and sprint toward ~$1B in revenue by 2028.

If the projections are even close, that ~77% forward growth is pretty eye-catching. Enphase gets its premium for the tech stack. Sunrun’s multiple comes from debt-heavy leases that promise steady cash while turning interest rates into a loaded gun. SunPower, with margins that thin, gives you a reason to squint.

Cash burn could increase if SunPower has trouble smoothly combining the businesses it's acquired. If that happens, the company may need to issue more shares through its equity line, which would dilute existing shareholders, especially at a time when the ITC changes could hurt demand and the overall solar market is expected to decline double digits.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I’m rating SPWR a Hold with speculative upside. The upside depends on a few things going right. SunPower is growing through acquisitions, gaining market share through TPO offerings, and benefiting from its very low valuation compared to peers. The downside comes from several risks, like weaker demand after the rush to buy ahead of ITC changes, already thin profit margins, possible problems integrating acquired companies, and the chance that SunPower may need to issue more shares through its equity line. I’d actually buy only on pullbacks or proof of durable post-ITC demand and margin expansion at SunPower versus Enphase and Sunrun.

data.title: SHYG: Less Upside From Compression
data.description:
Introduction & Thesis

In my last post on the iShares 0-5 Year High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (SHYG), I mentioned that the "hold" investment thesis for a six-month horizon was supported by a normalization of credit spreads (300 bp). On this occasion, the analytical framework remains unchanged. What has changed is the evolution of credit spreads, as the spread is currently around 240 bp.

This reduces the additional compression margin of the OAS itself and means that the expected return for the medium to long term will be more dominated by the ETF's carry. In this context, the relevant risk is not so much duration (which is low), but rather a cyclical episode of widening. These types of episodes occur when the market demands a higher risk premium. If that happens, the price of the ETF falls rapidly so that the carry takes time to offset that fall. Therefore, today's thesis remains to hold and buy only during episodes of spread widening, when the risk premium returns to "paying the tail" of credit.

To identify an episode of widening in credit spreads, we will have to see that the market is indeed beginning to pay more premiums for assumed risk. This can be clearly seen by monitoring Treasury bonds and credit spreads, which we will analyze later. To help the reader understand this better, carry is currently at acceptable levels, but there is a certain asymmetry between potential gains and losses at this time.

In terms of price, the upside potential is somewhat limited by the compression of credit spreads (240 bp). On the other hand, the downside potential is greater in terms of probability if a significant macroeconomic event occurs. In my view, the credit spread could compress somewhat further (50-60 bp), which could provide the fund with an extra 1-1.2% return, apart from the carry, but if a macroeconomic shock occurs, it would be quite feasible to see spreads widen by 200-300 bp. This means that the fund's price would fall by approximately 4%, which is why I continue to recommend holding and only buying when such a situation occurs.

ETF Overview

Analyzing the main characteristics of the ETF, we see what we discussed in the first section. Currently, the ETF provides a 30-day security yield of 6.24%, which is very decent in terms of carry. The effective duration is 1.89 years, which indicates that it is a fund that is not very sensitive to interest rate movements, and the OAS of corporate credit is 240.72 basis points. It has 1,148 securities in its portfolio and a 12-month trailing yield of 7.01%, which also speaks well for the consistency of the carry paid by the fund. Finally, it has annual associated expenses of 0.30%, as measured by the expense ratio published on its website.

The Portfolio

At the sector level, its holdings are distributed with a weighting of 19.21% in the cyclical consumer sector, followed by 14.07% in the communications sector and 12.46% in the non-cyclical consumer sector. As can be seen, at the sector level, the portfolio is constructed to achieve a relatively good balance between cyclical and defensive sectors.

The maturity structure of the bonds is organized as follows: bonds with maturities between 3 and 5 years (medium-term bonds) lead with 38.55%, followed by bonds with maturities between 0 and 1 year with a weight of 23.23% (ultra-short-term bonds). The 2-3 year maturity tranche represents 21.52%, and the 1-2 year tranche represents 14.80%.

The credit quality rating reflects, as the reader may have guessed, the type of asset to which this ETF is exposed. They buy sub-investment-grade and high-yield corporate bonds, which is why they offer such a competitive yield (carry). The fund is passively managed, seeking to replicate the performance of the Markit iBoxx USD Liquid High Yield 0-5 Index before fees. Currently, 48.94% is invested in corporate bonds with a BB credit rating, 33.47% in bonds with a B rating, and 13.66% in high-yield bonds with a CCC rating.

Payoff Profile

In terms of carry, the fund has effectively paid 7% over the last 12 months, according to data provided by Seeking Alpha. Dividend growth over the same period is 2.07%, partly due to falling interest rates. The last two items in the table also show positive data. The ETF has been paying a growing dividend for four years, which coincides with the monetary policy cycle imposed by the Federal Reserve in recent years, and has been paying dividends for 12 consecutive years, which tells us about its recurrence in carry payments and its usefulness as a tool for generating consistent income.

Macro Drivers

This last section helps us see what is happening with the drivers that influence the carry and the price of the fund. The first image shows us the evolution of the credit spread compared to the S&P 500, which allows us to see where equities stand in relation to this spread. The current situation is clearly risk-on, which confirms the previous statement that the market is paying less for the risk assumed. The second image shows us the curve regime between the 2-5 Y segment, which is where the fund currently stands. The colors indicate different curve regimes, classified as bear, bull, steeper, or flattener. Today, the 2-5 Y term spread has turned positive and is in a steepener twist regime, which means that recent credit movements have been somewhat more linked to the dynamics of interest rate cuts. This is why I maintain my hold thesis, with a call option in situations of widening. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Kraft Heinz: Berkshire May Be Out Of The Equation And What That Means For Shareholders
data.description:
Headaches for Kraft Heinz (KHC) shareholders keep on piling up as just very recently a regulatory filing revealed that the largest shareholder of the company - Berkshire Hathaway, Inc (BRK.B) intends to sell a part of the full amount of its stake within the company.

For those who have been following recent events surrounding the upcoming split of the company, including the rather unusual criticism by Buffett and his successor, the intention to sell is probably not a surprise.

The market, however, was caught off-guard by the regulatory filing and KHC share price reacted accordingly - falling by almost 6% on the following day.

As a result, KHC is now down 24% over the past year and almost 10% since my last warning that investors are better-off waiting for the split before taking action.

The fact that yesterday's share price movement was caused by a significant spike in trading volume that is well-above the 30-day average (41m against 15m) is a sign that share price volatility will likely persist in the coming days or even weeks. Speculations about the next move by the Oracle of Omaha could further exacerbate this problem even before any potential selling begins.

More Selling Pressure

The filing itself does not mean that Berkshire Hathaway intends to sell its entire stake in KHC, but the fact that it owns 27.5% of the company could have serious consequences for future returns, even if BRK.B trims a small portion of its stake.

A stake of this size would most likely be reduced over a number of quarters if not an entire year in order to avoid tanking the share price. Prior examples when BRK.B sold sizable amounts of shares in even larger business show that such transactions are carried out over multiple quarter and this puts consistent downward pressure to prices.

Bank of America (BAC) is one of the most recent examples. BRK.B's stake in the company was roughly 13% as of the end of Q2 2024. At that time BAC was trading at $40 apiece. In each of the following quarters, BRK.B went on sell BAC shares in each of the following quarters, up until the last reported one - Q3 of 2025. Although BAC is now trading 30% higher, the share price was trading sideways for many months following the first 13F release by BRK.B.

More importantly, the selling pressure was front-loaded during Q3 of 2024 which helps explain the share price volatility in that period and also why BAC significantly underperformed the sector in that quarter (see the second graph below).

Oracle (ORCL) is another example where BRK.B sold more than 40m shares in a single three-month period - during Q4 of 2018. The stake was much lower and the 40m shares represented around 1% ownership. The selling, however, coincided with a broader bear market in equities during the last quarter of 2018 so the overall impact is much harder to quantify. On top of that ORCL had a sizable share buyback program to the tune of $500m a year at that time which helped offset the selling pressure.

This brings the question of share buybacks and their role in cushioning the potential impact from a large shareholder selling activities. Kraft Heinz recently introduced share buyback program is roughly $900m per annum, while the current stake of BRK.B in the company is valued at around $7.8bn. Thus buybacks would definitely help, but will be insufficient to fully offset a potential exit by the Oracle of Omaha.

The Playbook

As I outlined back in November of last year, the best course of action when it comes to KHC is to wait for the company split in the second half of 2026. I still believe that BRK.B would most likely stick with the Global Taste Elevation Company which is also the better pick of the two, but all that is initial speculations and it will be best to await for the full information to become available after the split is complete.

When it comes to the recent regulatory filing by BRK.B, it doesn't change my thesis as any selling activity could be done in preparation for the split. More likely than not, however, we could see Berkshire beginning to trim its positions in Q1 of 2026 which based on the examples seen above would put pressure on the KHC share price. This once again confirms my view that the best approach for now is to wait and see.

data.title: Albertsons: Is This Stock Too Good To Be True?
data.description:
Albertsons (ACI) is a company that has never really interested me much. After all the confusion surrounding the failed merger with Kroger (KR), it seemed like a company without much appeal, lacking clear direction, and neither sufficiently asymmetric in valuation nor compelling enough in terms of quality to be considered an alternative buy to Costco (COST), Walmart (WMT), or even Kroger.

But I decided to revisit the case and listen to the Q3 earnings call, and what I saw ended up opening my eyes a little. The timing is still not good, and there will certainly still be challenges, but management is addressing the problems and the valuation is currently very attractive, by far the best among its major peers.

Therefore, even though there are uncertainties, I don't think this is a case of “too good to be true.” It doesn't seem to be a value trap, but rather a value play.

Albertsons: The Bad News First

Albertsons' Q3 earnings alone were not very encouraging. Management even called this quarter “solid,” with revenue increasing ~2% YoY. But even this low single-digit performance is not that encouraging, because it was driven by a reduction in the number of stores, in addition to lower fuel sales, which, although it may seem temporary, it is difficult to be very clear whether this is just a brief optimization of the portfolio or whether it stems from a more structural problem that will impact future expansion.

The margin was also not so good, with gross margin falling from 27.9% to 27.4%, and even removing some effects such as fuel, gross margin still fell by 0.5 percentage points due to the higher share of delivery and other higher costs. The good news is that SG&A decreased in relation to revenue due to operating leverage.

During the Q3 earnings call, much was said about inflation and other macroeconomic issues, attributing this more modest result to a difficult landscape, along with the company's initiatives to be more price efficient, as in the excerpt below:

“We're working very hard to soften the pass-through of inflation to our customers. So that said, we are pleased with the progress that we see in our price investments to date.”

However, this also has a competitive aspect. The excerpt above was from management responding to one of the analysts present at the earnings call who mentioned that other competitors are also making these price investments, and this reflects the current scenario well. The peers mentioned, Costco, Walmart, Kroger, all have a larger scale and can better absorb costs. For example, Costco's very low gross margin, which is deliberately lower than that of its peers, is much debated in the market.

But note two points in the table below. The first is that ACI's gross margin is the highest among the peers I mentioned earlier, which, despite being a positive point for the business they have structured, is also a point of attention, as there is more room to decrease and enter the average of this competitive landscape. A second point is efficiency: even with lower margins, all other players deliver higher EBITDA and EBIT margins.

Furthermore, considering this structure, we have companies such as Costco and Walmart expanding. Companies such as Amazon (AMZN) and Mercado Libre (MELI) are entering the grocery delivery market more aggressively, and all of this increases uncertainty about the future. If Albertsons is currently struggling to stand out in the market, in 10 years' time, there may be even greater pressure. Think locally: if Albertsons is stronger in a certain region today, that may change in a few years if the company continues to decrease or stabilize its number of stores while its competitors are more aggressive. And even though we have some level of loyalty with millions of loyalty members, this “stickiness” is not as high as that of a Costco.

Another point that is not so interesting in the case today is the debt in relation to free cash flow. Net debt is at $15.2 billion, which seems manageable, but in the first nine months of 2025, operating cash flow was $1.6 billion, and CapEx was over $1.4 billion, meaning free cash flow was quite compressed. So even though the capital structure is not super concerning, it is another yellow flag.

Albertsons Is Solving These Problems

The good part is that I heard exactly what I wanted to hear on the earnings call. Even with a low valuation, allowing for a high shareholder yield, the sentiment was that the number one priority is not valuation (although it is one of the priorities), but rather the transformation and restructuring of the business.

This connects to the previous point about free cash flow being pressured by still-high CapEx, even with the number of stores decreasing, precisely because Albertsons is investing heavily in several areas. One of the points frequently mentioned by management recently was investments in tech, such as what they called “AI transformation,” which were made to improve Albertsons' digital side, and the company is already reaping the rewards. It was mentioned that “Ask AI” feature increased customer basket size by about 10%, and in addition, one of the points that “saved” Q3 was digital sales, which grew 21%, and loyalty members, which increased 12% YoY.

AI also helps with the pricing issue I mentioned in the section above. Competition is a factor that drives prices down, increasing price wars, but with investment in tech, the company is able to do this much more accurately, so I see this as a positive.

In addition, physical infrastructure, such as the 23 remodels carried out in Q3, is another factor that significantly increases CapEx, not to mention other factors such as investment to increase private labels.

What I mean here is that the case is not as simple as “Albertsons is losing ground... and free cash flow is under pressure.” It's more like, yes, Albertsons is not in a good place right now, and free cash flow is a little under pressure because the company is investing heavily to remain competitive.

Then... Shareholder Value Remains

It seems quite feasible to me that in a few years, let's say in 2030, these initiatives will be much more mature, with new stores providing a more acceptable return, consolidated margins increasing due to increased efficiency in AI, and revenue growth at a healthier level with potential improvements in pricing.

It is still a case full of uncertainties, and we need to see very disciplined execution by management, but still, it seems very feasible to me that after the strategies mature and this more turbulent time passes, there will be many benefits for shareholders.

Think about it with me. Today, the P/E ratio is 8x. Kroger, which is the second lowest multiple, is at 13x. I don't think there will be a rerating to a “Walmart-like” level, with ACI trading at 30x earnings or more, but I think it's feasible to think that after getting back on track, the market will see potential and the company will trade at something closer to 15x.

Another point worth considering is future EPS. The market itself already estimates a recovery for fiscal year 2027, with a slight improvement in business and buybacks, with estimates at $2.30. Today, ACI stock is trading at $17. We are saying that if the company distributes all of this in dividends/buybacks, the yield is 13%. But don't consider this the base scenario, because there is still a lot to be done.

However, a scenario that is not so impossible is for EPS to be close to ~$2.60 in 2029 or 2030, with initiatives already well mature, and the company choosing to distribute 50% or 60% of this, which would still give a high single-digit yield.

The Bottom Line

Well, I started the analysis with the question of whether Albertsons' case was too good to be true, and I don't think that's really the case. It's an honest investment thesis. There are uncertainties, there will be significant challenges, but at the same time, these risks do not make the case unfeasible. In fact, with this valuation at such a depressed level, there is a huge margin of safety.

Therefore, I think the current risk reward is attractive, and my rating for ACI stock is a buy.

data.title: Northern Trust: Strong Stock Markets Continue To Pad Earnings
data.description:
Rampant global stock markets haven’t just been great for investors’ portfolios; they have also been a boon for custody banks’ earnings. Chicago's Northern Trust (NTRS) hasn't missed out, with buoyant asset-based fees helping the bank's earnings per share up nearly 20% last year. As you would expect, that has been rocket fuel for Northern's stock. Shares have returned nearly 40% this past year, more than 20 points better than the S&P 500.

Writing about the company back in October, I did say that my only major problem was the valuation. With the shares up around 20% since then, Northern has only gotten more expensive, leaving its price-to-book ratio well beyond its historical average. With that partly resting on historically expensive stock markets, I would wait for a pullback before buying.

Strong Operating Leverage

Global stock markets delivered strong performances last year. While international equities led the way, the S&P 500 still returned around 18%, marking its third straight year of double-digit gains.

With around half of its custodied assets sitting in equities, this has been a boon for Northern's asset-based servicing revenue. The bank booked $496.4 million in custody & fund administration fees last quarter, up 9% year-on-year.

Of course, Northern is more than just a custody bank. It is also a global wealth and investment manager. Equities accounted for over 55% of Northern’s assets under management last quarter, meaning buoyant stock markets have also been a tailwind for this part of its business. Indeed, Northern generated $578 million in Wealth fees last quarter, up 6% year-on-year.

Asset-based revenue isn't the only thing that’s been strong for Northern recently. Net interest income, which was around 30% of the bank’s revenue last quarter, has also been at a cyclically strong level. Like many banks, Northern has benefited from cheaper funding costs and fixed-asset repricing in its bond portfolio. Its overall asset yield is still falling in the face of lower short-term interest rates, but it has lost less from this process than it has gained from lower borrowing yields.

As a result, net interest income grew a strong 10% sequentially last quarter, with Northern’s net interest margin expanding by 11 basis points to 1.81%. That is the widest it has been in over a decade.

Because there aren't really any incremental expenses associated with these trends, they have been great for operating leverage. Northern's adjusted revenue growth outpaced underlying cost growth by 400 basis points last quarter – one reason why its earnings growth has been so impressive of late.

I would make a similar point regarding capital and profitability. Because its earnings growth is coming from capital-light activity (higher asset-based fee revenue and an expanding net interest margin), it has been highly accretive to Northern's return on equity. The bank recorded a 15.4% return on equity last quarter, which was roughly 200 basis points above its prior 5-year average.

Soaring Shareholder Payouts

Good news isn’t limited to the income statement. Northern’s balance sheet is also in a strong position right now, with the bank sitting on a 12.6% Common Equity Tier 1 ratio at the end of 2025. This is comfortably ahead of management’s 11-12% target range.

Coupled with its strong profit growth, high ROE, and relatively low reinvestment needs, this is supporting excellent capital returns to its common stockholders. Northern spent just under $1.9 billion on dividends and buybacks last year, around 25% more than in 2024. Of course, the buyback component is helping the bank's share-level bottom line. Average diluted shares fell 5% in 2025 - a second reason why Northern's earnings growth has been so impressive.

Initial 2026 guidance implies this may continue. While operating leverage is seen moderating sharply, management still thinks it can be over 100 basis points this year, while the bank's payout ratio is seen above 100%. Given Northern's current market value is around $27 billion, investors are looking at an attractive high single-digit forward shareholder yield.

Premium Valuation

While everything looks great for Northern right now, the main issue is that this is reflected in its valuation. Its stock trades for $150 as I type, which is up from around $108 a year ago. That move has seen Northern’s price-to-book multiple expand by over 30% in that time. At just over 2.3 times, it’s now running at a circa 20% premium to its 10-year average.

Northern has grown its book value at a circa 6% annualized rate over that period. It also offers a 2.1% dividend yield. Together, that creates a reasonable expectation of an 8%-9% long-term annualized return. My main concern is this: with Northern’s price-to-book ratio at a premium to its past average, mean reversion would be a headwind to equity returns, eating into whatever Northern delivers from book value growth and dividend.

Now, Northern’s premium price-to-book multiple reflects the fact that its return on equity is strong right now. However, this is resting to a degree on elevated equity markets. As you can see in the chart above, valuations for the S&P 500 look stretched by historical standards. Any correction would weigh on Northern’s ability to generate positive operating leverage, its ROE, and its current level of capital returns. That would likely have a negative knock-on impact on the stock. For that reason, I would like to see Northern drop below its long-run price-to-book ratio before being a buyer. Accordingly, I continue to rate shares "Hold."

data.title: TOYO: Made In USA Scale And Ethiopia Cost Edge Make The Stock A Strong Buy
data.description:
When I first wrote about TOYO Co., Ltd. (TOYO), I showed that this vertically integrated solar manufacturer had a history of stock market outperformance since its July 2024 IPO, thanks to rapid expansion and strategic acquisitions. The scaling of its cost-advantaged Ethiopia plant, the redirecting of Vietnam capacity toward non-U.S. markets, and the ramping up of its U.S. module production to benefit from "made-in-USA" demand and incentives, were sources of strength. Although margins and growth had softened around the time of my thesis due to ramp-up costs and shifting market mix, commentary from management suggested that there would be a rebound as new capacity and the VSUN brand integration matured. With the company trading at an attractive valuation compared to its peers, there was significant upside assuming profitability bounced back. This made the stock a "Strong Buy" for me. TOYO only files half-year and annual reports, so there have been no updates to the company's financial results since my first article. However, there have been developments regarding its U.S. operations and the integration of its VSUN brand that reinforce my original rating.

Market-Beating Stock Performance

Since that first article in September of last year, the stock has gone up 7.35% compared to total shareholder returns ("TSR") of 4.62% for the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and 5.01% for the iShares Russell 3000 ETF (IWV). This continues the company's history as a market-beater.

U.S. Plant Moves to Commercial Production

I noted in that first article that TOYO's November 2024 acquisition of Solar Plus Technology Texas LLC to bolster its U.S. presence brought with it a "567,140 sq. ft. Houston-area site with an initial 2.5 GW solar module capacity, expandable to 6.5 GW by 2029". Phase 1 of the project was completed in early 2025, with management projecting 1GW of solar output by mid-2025, which would scale to 2.5GW by the end of 2025. Trial production commenced upon acquisition. In October, management announced that it had begun commercial production, saying that,

"During the trial production phase, the Company has received strong demand from its U.S. clients for the modules manufactured at the Houston Facility. As operations scale, TOYO anticipates robust demand from utility-scale developers seeking reliable and domestically manufactured modules that qualify for favorable tax incentives."

Moreover, the company's chairman and CEO, Junsei Ryu, reiterated that commercial production was "a major step forward in our 'made-in-USA-for-the-USA' strategy". Production in the U.S., is, according to management,

"expected to qualify for tax incentives under Section 45X of the Internal Revenue Code, providing up to $0.07 per watt in production credits through 2030. This strengthens TOYO's cost competitiveness while supporting broader goals for domestic energy manufacturing."

It needs reiterating that solar manufacturing is dominated by the Chinese, whose economies of scale are unmatched. The Production Tax Credit (PTC) for solar energy gives producers a base credit of $0.0275 per kilowatt-hour for solar electricity they generate. By subsidizing domestic production rather than installation, the incentives directly improve TOYO's unit economics while creating a sustained competitive advantage against imported modules that cannot access similar benefits. Furthermore, a side effect of changes to the PTC in the One Big Beautiful Bill ("OBBB) Act is that they give existing producers a competitive advantage over market entrants. Under those changes, solar producers must have been placed in service by December 31, 2027, or construction must have begun by July 4, 2026.

Management continues to believe that the combination of robust domestic demand and constrained domestic supply, presents a unique opportunity. Unsaid in this is that the combination of the two supports higher prices and therefore TOYO's efforts to improve its profitability.

TOYO Has Further Deepened Its Ties With the U.S.

In addition, management plans on establishing a R&D center in the United States, further building out its supply chain in the country. In early December last year, TOYO announced that it had agreed to procure critical raw materials from a U.S. polysilicon supplier, further aligning its supply chain with American policy priorities. In December 2025, TOYO acquired the remaining stake in TOYO Solar LLC, its American subsidiary which was already engaged in advanced development of a 2.5 GW solar module manufacturing facility. In the words of chairman and CEO Junsei Ryu,

"this acquisition marks a key milestone in our U.S. growth strategy. Full ownership of TOYO Solar LLC strengthens our operational control, aligns our long-term interests, and reinforces our commitment to delivering American-made solar solutions at scale."

These moves serve to create a more robust and capable supply chain and aid in TOYO's Made-in-America plans, ensuring shorter delivery cycles and reduced logistics costs. Collectively, these features make TOYO more competitive. The presence of TOYO across different segments of the market and American value chain adds gravitas to this position.

Management Projects a Record 2025

In a November 2025 investor deck, management projected an FY2025 with its biggest solar cell shipments and net income levels ever. On the back of solar cell shipments rising from 1.74 gigawatts ("GW") in 2024 to between 4.2 GW and 4.4 GW in 2025, management expects revenue to reach between $375 million and $400 million, and net income to leap from $5.4 million in 2024 to between $39 million to $45 million in 2025. Importantly, these estimates do not take into account contributions from the U.S. facility, so are conservative.

Ethiopia is Driving Growth

Driving growth in solar cell shipments and profitability is the firm's N-Type cell line 4 GW annual capacity plant in Hawass, Ethiopia. Since the April 2025 installation of the 2 GW facility, production has now reached the full allocation of 4 GW. Just as with the U.S., TOYO is able to benefit from favorable investment policies, as well as the country's competitive power rates and abundant supply of hydropower. The Ethiopian plant has orders for the whole of the year, providing shipment visibility.

The Ethiopian plant can serve the U.S. market, given that Ethiopia is subject to a 10% tariff and does not fall under any anti-dumping or countervailing duties. The scale of the facility gives TOYO the ability to respond aggressively to new demand around the world, while benefitting from a low-cost region.

With TOYO's Vietnam operations now facing constrained U.S. market access, the Ethiopian capacity serves as both a production hedge and a market access vehicle, allowing the company to redirect its Vietnam output to non-U.S. markets while serving American customers from Ethiopia.

VSUN Acquisition Extending Integrated Value Chain

The VSUN brand acquisition has proven instrumental in TOYO's market penetration strategy. VSUN's established relationships with U.S. utility-scale developers and its reputation for quality have provided TOYO with immediate credibility in the American market. This brand recognition complements TOYO's manufacturing capabilities, allowing the company to leverage existing customer relationships while introducing its cost-advantaged production methods. The integration has streamlined go-to-market efforts significantly.

Policy-Driven Market Segmentation Continues

As Chinese producers continue expanding capacity despite softening demand growth rates, there is a supply glut that benefits installers but pressures manufacturers.

At the same time, policy-driven market segmentation is emerging, with the U.S. increasingly favoring domestic suppliers while European markets implement their own local preferences. This fragmentation creates opportunities for strategically positioned manufacturers like TOYO to capture premium pricing in protected markets.

Navigating this evolving landscape is crucial for a solar solutions company such as TOYO, whose geographic diversification allows it to tap into various growing markets.

This process of adaptation has not been without pain. For instance, in the second half of 2024, imports from Southeast Asia faced anti-dumping and countervailing duties investigations. Currently, the individual anti-dumping rate for Vietnam is between 54.46% and 271.28% and the preliminary countervailing duty rate is 2.85%. This effectively acts as a disincentive for importing Vietnamese solar solutions into the U.S. While management has redirected Vietnamese cell capacity to India and Taiwan, while meeting U.S. demand from Ethiopia, it is notable that India's solar cell market is also undergoing a supply glut, as anti-dumping and countervailing duty investigations lead manufacturers to India.

Policy Changes Could Upend U.S. Economics

TOYO's single biggest threat is a policy and trade regime shift that removes its structural advantage in the U.S. market. The U.S. is currently in a revolution regarding its trade policy, and with midterms this year, there is ample incentive for the government to accelerate changes in the event they lose the midterms. Moreover, currently, the Trump Administration is waiting for a ruling on the legality of tariffs. While the president has indicated that it will use other presidential powers to maintain the tariff level, the possibility that the U.S. plant can not only manufacture efficiently, but do so within a protected profit pool created by American industrial policy, may be hurt by the fall of tariffs. Nevertheless, U.S. industrial policy as a whole will remain, with its production-linked incentives, so many favorable protections will remain for U.S. based producers.

U.S. policy could change regarding manufacturing credits, which could be further narrowed, delayed, challenged, or rendered less valuable in practice, hurting TOYO's ability to improve its profitability. At the same time, TOYO's Ethiopian plant, currently positioned as a tariff-manageable, and an ant-dumping/countervailing duties-safe supply source into the U.S., is inherently vulnerable to reclassification risk. A broadening of anti-circumvention enforcement, extension of duties to new jurisdictions, or tightening what qualifies as "domestic" content could simultaneously impair TOYO's market access and compress realized pricing. This would force TOYO's operating model back into the global commodity arena, where oversupply and aggressive Chinese pricing set the clearing price, and where scale alone is not enough to defend margins. Unable to reallocate factories or quickly reconfigure as policy changes, this creates downside. Without a continuity of existing rules, or favourable policy changes, TOYO could suffer tremendously. That said, both Republicans and Democrats have largely embraced the idea of protecting domestic manufacturers and reshoring production, so while the type of policies that will exist at the end of the year or at the end of the Trump Administration, or the end of the succeeding administration, may be uncertain, neither party has a free market wing in the ascendancy that would push out industrial policy. With TOYO already through the door, the most catastrophic policy options are unlikely to affect them badly.

Valuation Offers Further Upside

At the current price, TOYO has a price/earnings multiple of 9.88, compared to TOYO seems undervalued compared to Seeking Alpha's 32.71 sector median, and Aswath Damodaran's sector median of 44.56. This seems to be a reflection of the market's skepticism around its ability to sustain its profitability. Historically, the company's profitability has swung wildly, making it hard to predict profits for the next year. Without that smoothness of earnings, analysts and investors have been cautious about making predictions on the company. The market has not properly accounted for the impact of the firm's integration into the U.S. market, and so, has not re-rated the firm for a period of more sustainable and higher profits. As a result, TOYO is grossly undervalued. If we assume that earnings per share ("EPS") remain at current levels, and the P/E multiple converges on peers, then TOYO has a target price of $24.73 and an upside of nearly 294% from current levels.

Strong Buy

In light of industry developments, the initial thesis supporting TOYO as a value-driven buy persists. The company has demonstrated strong execution, deftly navigating a dynamic geopolitical terrain while investing strategically in new markets. With 2026 capacity targets on track and the Houston facility ramping production, TOYO appears well-positioned to capitalize on U.S. demand for domestically manufactured modules. The combination of geographic diversification, expanding production capacity, and improving margins as facilities mature supports continued outperformance potential.
